text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Amelia Shah
Riverside Medical Practice

12 Garden Row, Southwark, London SE1 7AB
Dear Dr Amelia Shah
Alex Turner, DOB: 14-03-2003, Hospital No: H9283746 NHS No. 7410956332
Flat 3, 22 Mill Street, London SE1 2BD

Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent seizures of uncertain classification with ongoing events despite lifestyle modification efforts; prolonged screen exposure and irregular sleep pattern noted in context of university esports leadership role. Previous normal neurological examination in clinic; prior CT head (2021) reportedly normal per patient.

Previous medication: Levetiracetam (behavioural irritability), sodium valproate (stopped due to weight gain and patient preference), carbamazepine (dizziness), lamotrigine (rash), clobazam PRN (sedation), lacosamide (no clear benefit), perampanel (mood changes), topiramate (cognitive slowing), zonisamide (poor appetite), brivaracetam (sleep disturbance).

Not taken: pregabalin, tiagabine, rufinamide, cannabidiol, cenobamate.

Comorbidities and relevant history: Migraine without aura (intermittent), myopia, no known cardiac disease, no family history of epilepsy reported. Sleep restriction related to competitive gaming and tournament scheduling. Caffeine use moderate to high during exam periods.

I reviewed Alex in clinic today. He attended alone and provided a seizure diary covering the last 8 weeks. He describes sudden brief losses of postural control with rapid recovery, occurring as drop attacks twice a day on average, more commonly in the late evening after extended matches or during periods of sleep deprivation. He reports occasional brief staring spells noted by teammates on voice chat, where he becomes unresponsive for several seconds, but he is unsure of the frequency as these are not always witnessed in person. There is no clear prodrome, though he sometimes experiences a "pressure" sensation behind the eyes. He denies tongue biting or prolonged confusion. There have been two minor head knocks when collapsing onto a chair; no emergency attendances. He is currently leading the university esports club and often streams late into the night, with variable sleep length (4–6 hours on weekdays). Alcohol intake is minimal; no recreational drugs. He uses blue-light filters but has not trialled structured screen breaks.

Examination today: Neurological examination normal. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. BP 118/74, pulse 68 regular. No orthostatic drop. No tremor. Gait normal.

Current medication: None for seizures at present. Uses ibuprofen occasionally for headaches and melatonin purchased over-the-counter on some nights (advised to avoid unsupervised use).

Investigations: We have no recent EEG or MRI on record. He reports a CT head in 2021 (sports concussion clinic) as normal. I have requested retrieval of that report. Risk discussion held regarding injury with sudden drops; he currently wears a soft beanie but no protective headgear.

Plan:
- Safety: We discussed injury prevention given drop attacks twice a day; advised padding/sharp-edge protection at gaming desk, consideration of a soft protective headband for training sessions, and not standing during prolonged online matches. He will arrange a buddy system during travel between late sessions.
- Triggers and routine: Patient plans to implement fixed sleep–wake times (target 7–8 hours), scheduled screen breaks (20 minutes off every 2 hours), hydration plan, and reduced caffeine after 14:00. He will log adherence alongside events in the seizure diary.
- Investigations: Arrange MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and routine EEG with photic stimulation and hyperventilation; consider ambulatory EEG depending on results. Baseline bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, vitamin D) requested.
- Treatment options discussed: He is keen to review choices after investigations. We discussed potential re-trial of a well-tolerated agent versus alternatives not yet used. Side effects and SUDEP risk were outlined, alongside DVLA regulations. He is not currently driving and understands to remain off driving until event-free per guidance.
- University liaison: With consent, I will provide a brief note to the university wellbeing service and esports coordinator to support reasonable adjustments (earlier match scheduling, rest periods, safe environment setup).
- Follow-up: Nurse specialist telephone review in 6 weeks to monitor event frequency, safety adherence, and investigation progress. Clinic review in 3 months or sooner if events escalate, injury occurs, or prolonged post-event confusion develops.

I will write again when results are available. Please let me know if there are any concerns or if there is deterioration.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: drop attacks twice a day on average

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides explicit frequency: "drop attacks twice a day on average." It also mentions occasional brief staring spells with uncertain frequency, but since their frequency is not quantified, we cannot add them. Per the rule to select the highest frequency and sum only when frequencies are known and in the same dimension, we use the explicit drop attack frequency. Convert "twice a day" to the allowed format "2 per day."
seizure_frequency_number: drop attacks twice a day on average
